Abstract
--------

Aging is a complex and multifaceted process involving a variety of interrelated molecular mechanisms and cellular systems. Phenotypically, the biological aging process is accompanied by a gradual loss of cellular function and the systemic deterioration of multiple tissues, resulting in susceptibility to aging-related diseases. Emerging evidence suggests that aging is closely associated with telomere attrition, DNA damage, mitochondrial dysfunction, loss of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels, impaired macro-autophagy, stem cell exhaustion, inflammation, loss of protein balance, deregulated nutrient sensing, altered intercellular communication, and dysbiosis. These age-related changes may be alleviated by intervention strategies, such as calorie restriction, improved sleep quality, enhanced physical activity, and targeted longevity genes. In this review, we summarise the key historical progress in the exploration of important causes of aging and anti-aging strategies in recent decades, which provides a basis for further understanding of the reversibility of aging phenotypes, the application prospect of synthetic biotechnology in anti-aging therapy is also prospected.

**Keywords:** Aging, Aging triggers, Synthetic, Senolytic, Anti-aging strategies

Background
----------

Aging will be a major social problems worldwide in the coming decades [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR1)–[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR3)]. During the aging process, the body tissues and organs of the older people undergo functional decline or deterioration, thus increasing their susceptibility to age-related diseases and shortening their healthy life span, which has brought enormous financial pressure to countries worldwide in terms of pension, medical expenses, and health care [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR4)–[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR6)]. Therefore, exploring the biological nature of aging, searching for safe and effective intervention strategies to positively regulate health status, and prolonging the healthy lifespan of the aging population are important for reducing the global pension burden and promoting healthy aging.

Aging is a progressive degenerative state that can be physiological and pathological [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR7)–[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR9)] (Fig.[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#Fig1)). Physiological aging is observed in across many species, and is a degenerative process that occurs after maturation, including telomere attrition [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR10), [11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR11)], DNA damage [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR12), [13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR13)], mitochondrial dysfunction [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR14), [15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR15)], loss of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD+) levels [[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR16), [17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR17)], impaired macro-autophagy [[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR18), [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR19)], stem cell exhaustion, inflammation [[20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR20), [21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR21)], loss of protein balance [[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR22)], deregulated nutrient-sensing [[23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR23)], altered intercellular communication [[24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR24)–[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR26)] and dysbiosis [[27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR27), [28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR28)], thereby leading to systemic functional decline. Importantly, these changes are decentralised and interactive, not independent of each other. Pathological aging includes the senile pathological aging changes, which are caused by various external factors, such as cardiovascular disease [[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR29)], cerebrovascular disease [[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR30)], degenerative joint disease [[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR31), [32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR32)], diabetes [[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR33)], Parkinson’s disease [[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR34), [35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR35)], Alzheimer’s disease [[36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR36)], cancer [[37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR37)–[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR39)], and degeneration of multiple organ functions. These aging-induced cellular physiological and pathological changes can reflect the underlying nutrient sensing, intercellular communication, protein stabilisation, epigenetics, and molecular abnormalities in DNA damage repair, leading to genomic instability and damage. Further understanding of the different molecular mechanisms involved in the aging process is of great importance for preventing aging and prolonging the lifespan.

### Fig. 1.

[![Image 9: Fig. 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/84ee/11118732/4242ebc8d6b3/12964_2024_1663_Fig1_HTML.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=11118732_12964_2024_1663_Fig1_HTML.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/figure/Fig1/)

Aging drivers and age-related diseases. Major physiological features of aging include NAD+ loss, telomeres attrition, mitochondrial dysfunction, stem cell exhaustion, disabled macro-autophagy, DNA damage, protein balance loss, inflammation, dysbiosis, deregulated nutrient sensing, and altered cellular communication. These physiological characteristics of aging are primitive, antagonistic, and integrated, and their interaction promotes aging. When aging reaches a certain threshold, organ and tissue function continues to deteriorate, which increases the incidence and mortality of aging-related diseases, including cardiovascular, cerebrovascular, degenerative joint disease, diabetes, Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, and cancer

In recent years, a large number of animal and clinical experiments have been conducted to study factors that induce aging, such as morphological and pathological changes and functional decline of the aging organism. Indeed, some differences between biological and chronological age reflect the validity of age-accelerated or deceleration procedures, which are well-known biomarkers of the aging process. Researchers have gradually expanded from traditional methods of measuring aging (including maximal energy expenditure at the respiratory, sensory, psychomotor, and cognitive levels) to modern biotechnological methods, such as genomics, epigenomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics. These techniques may have implications for assessing the spatiotemporal patterns of health degradation and effectiveness of anti-aging strategies.

Briefly, aging is a complex process, and its characteristics are interdependent. Each of these factors should be considered as an entry point for future exploration of the aging process and the development of novel life extensions. Here, we review the history and current state of aging research and summarise the characteristics of aging and the mechanisms promoting aging. In addition, we review different types of aging mechanisms and their corresponding anti-aging strategies. This knowledge can guide the design of preventive and therapeutic strategies to delay aging and age-related diseases and extend human health and longevity.

Potential triggers and molecular mechanisms of aging
----------------------------------------------------

Aging is a complex result of many biological processes, and many key factors trigger aging, such as DNA damage, telomere dysregulation, mitochondrial dysregulation, NAD+ loss, autophagy disorders, and stem cell exhaustion. Here, we summarise the main causes and underlying molecular mechanisms contributing to the aging process.

### Aging and DNA damage

DNA damage is a major internal factor that leads to genomic instability, epigenetic changes, protein stress, impaired mitochondrial function, and telomere dysfunction [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR12)]. The continuous accumulation of DNA-damaged cells triggers cell death and senescence, ultimately leading to chronic inflammation, loss of function, atrophy, and disease in cells and tissues [[40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR40)].

#### Molecular mechanisms of DNA damage

Genomic instability manifests as permanent and transmissible changes in DNA sequence [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR13), [41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR41)]. DNA damage caused by an inherently unstable genome includes spontaneous deamination, hydrolysis, and many other chemical changes such as different types of breaks, changes in base positions, gaps, DNA-protein cross-links, and other subtle chemical modifications [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR12), [42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR42)]. Abnormal DNA structures (e.g. G-quadruplexes, R-loops, and persistent single-stranded regions), as well as abnormal intermediates in DNA transactions (e.g. stalled transcription, replication, and recombination complexes), are considered phenotypes of DNA damage [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR13)]. Genomic mutations caused by DNA instability adversely affect cellular functions and are major causes of cancer and genetic diseases. However, DNA instability is also the most important substrate in the evolution of species [[43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR43), [44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR44)]. DNA integrity is maintained by the continuous repair of highly complex DNA repair and DNA damage response (DDR) systems that counteract the time-dependent erosion and destruction of genetic DNA information. Progressive telomere shortening is another major contributor to DNA damage that accelerates the aging process [[45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR45)].

DNA damage is the major driver of age-related epigenetic changes. The epigenome, which includes DNA methylation and histone modifications, is unstable throughout the life cycle of somatic cells [[46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR46)]. DNA damage leads to persistent chromatin changes that enrich aging-enhancing DNA fragments (DNA-scars) in senescent cells [[47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR47)]. Persistent DNA damage and repair-related cellular physiological effects may leave epigenetic marks, resulting in epigenetic heterogeneity among cells. Transcription appears to change considerably more in senescent cells than in young cells. Thus, DDR may be a major cause of epigenetic changes that impair gene expression control, leading to somatic heterogeneity and a time-dependent decline in overall function.

#### Relationship between DNA damage and aging

During aging, numerous exogenous and endogenous genotoxins, photoaging, and mechanical stress in tissues continuously induce DNA damage (Fig.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#Fig2)). Approximately 10 5 DNA damage events occur in mammalian cells every day, although most of the DNA damage is effectively excised or repaired. Notably, a small portion escapes the DNA damage detection and repair system, subsequently resulting in failure to repair or repair errors [[48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR48)]. Many studies using mammalian models have confirmed an inextricable link between DNA damage and aging [[49](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR49)–[52](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR52)]. As aging progresses, the DNA repair capacity gradually declines, and the increased molecular phenotype of genomic instability becomes the main marker of aging. Markers of DNA damage are found in patients with age-related diseases such as cardiovascular disease [[53](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR53)], Alzheimer’s disease [[54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR54)], and cancer [[55](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR55)], suggesting that DNA damage is directly related to the incidence of these diseases. Patients with genetic or acquired defects in DNA repair proteins also exhibit features of premature aging and that differences in the location of the defect in the DNA repair system can lead to premature aging in different organs [[56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR56)]. Specifically, RecQ helicase plays an important role in DNA recombination, replication, repair, and telomere maintenance, and its mutation may increase the incidence of Werner, Bloom, and Rothmund-Thomson syndromes [[57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR57)]. Global genome nucleotide excision repair deficiency leads to a thousand-fold increase in skin cancer susceptibility and may accelerate neurodegeneration [[58](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR58)]. Impaired transcription-coupled repair mechanisms can cause typical age-related pathologies, such as neurodegeneration, osteoporosis, and atherosclerosis [[59](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR59)]. Hutchinson-Gilford progeria is associated with nuclear genome instability, defects in DNA double-strand break repair leading to telangiectasia and Nijmegen break syndrome, and defects in DNA cross-linking repair leading to anaemia [[60](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR60)]. In addition, DNA damage caused by mitochondrial defects is another underlying factor in a class of progressive diseases that affect multiple organs.

Drivers of DNA damage and the resulting systemic consequences. The nuclear and mitochondrial genomes are constantly exposed to exogenous substances (such as ultraviolet and X-rays, chemicals in food, water, and air), endogenous substances such as ROS, advanced glycation end products (AGEs), and aldehydes; this results in genetic abnormalities, including mutation, deletion, aneuploidy, translocation, dysfunctional telomeres, epigenetic alterations, and mitochondrial dysfunction. DNA damage and DNA damage response caused by the above factors can shock molecular processes and alter cell fate, such as cell death, senescence, and systemic breakdown of repair functions, eventually leading to the loss of cell and organ function and promoting the occurrence and development of age-related diseases

Overall, defects in the DNA damage repair system directly leads to the continued accumulation of genomic mutations, which underlie many segmental forms of premature aging in humans, suggesting a close link between genome integrity and aging. Although considerable progress has been achieved in the study of the mechanistic connection between DNA damage and aging, there are still many issues to be further explore the specific molecular mechanisms by which DNA damage affects diseases in older people. Therefore, fundamentally addressing the aging process and combating age-related diseases are important for exploring the relationship between DNA damage and anti-aging effects.

### Aging and telomere attrition

Telomeres are small stretches of DNA-protein complexes present at the ends of linear chromosomes in eukaryotic cells, which maintain chromosomal integrity, control the cell division cycle, and are essential for an organism’s healthy life span and reproduction [[61](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR61)]. As early as the 1960s, a scientist named Leonardo Hayflick discovered that cultured human fibroblasts had limited and reproducible replication capacity and were governed by cell-autonomous mechanisms [[62](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR62), [63](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR63)]. Even if the cold stops the cell division, once the temperature rises again, the cells will continue to divide before freezing, until 50 times after the cessation of division. Heverick realized that cells have a deep-seated internal mechanism that controls the number of times they divide [[64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR64)]. In the 1970s, Olovnikov [[65](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR65)] and Watson [[66](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR66)] discovered the “end duplication problem” by looking at asymmetries in linear DNA replication and predicting that each cell division results in chromosomal DNA at the ends of the lagging strands loss, eventually leading to the gradual shortening of chromosomes. Limited telomere length reserve is an obstacle to cell proliferation and viability, and the loss of telomere function is closely associated with age-related functional decline and increased incidence of disease [[67](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR67)] (Fig.[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#Fig3)A).

#### Telomere and telomerase structure

Telomere end protection is evolutionarily highly conserved from lower to higher multicellular organisms [[68](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR68)]. Structurally, telomeres consist of repeating nucleotide sequences 3’-[TTAGGG]-5’ in tandem, ranging from a few to tens of bases, terminated at the 3’ end by a single strand of guanine-rich nucleotides of 75 to 300 nt, forming a “cap structure” (Fig.[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#Fig3)B). Telomeres are covered by a special protein called the shelterin complex, which is a multimer of six protein subunits (TRF1, TRF2, TPP1, POT1, TIN2, and RAP1) that work together to protect the chromosomes and regulate telomere length [[68](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR68)]. Telomeres and shelterin complexes form a sophisticated higher-order structure that protects DNA repair programmes from fusing ends by mediating non-homologous end-joining of telomeric DNA through double-stranded DNA break detection, ultimately involved in the capping, protection, and regulation of telomeres [[69](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR69)]. Correspondingly, mutations in these six protein components can disrupt the shelterin-telomere complex, resulting in terminal fusion and premature senescence. Specifically, telomere maintenance is inseparable from normal expression of TRF1 [[70](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR70), [71](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR71)]. TRF1 deletion induces telomeric DNA to form a fragile site phenotype, whereas TRF1 overexpression impairs telomerase binding to telomere ends, eventually resulting in telomere shortening [[72](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR72), [73](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR73)]. TRF2 folds telomeric DNA into T-loops, inhibits the ataxia telangiectasia mutated-dependent DDR at chromosome ends, and suppresses end-to-end chromosome fusion and canonical homologous end joining [[74](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR74)]. In addition, TIN2 plays a connecting role in the shelterin complex and forms bridges between different shelterin proteins [[75](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR75)]. TIN2 mutations do not interfere with the spatial structure of other shelterin components on telomeres; however, the TIN2-R282H mutation activates telomeric DNA damage signalling, which results in telomere instability associated with telomerase activity, eventually leading to a premature cellular senescence phenotype [[76](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR76)]. Uncontrolled POT1 impairs telomerase binding to telomere ends, resulting in shortened telomeres [[77](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR77)]. TPP1 interacts with telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT) to recruit telomerase and its loss elicits a robust telomeric DNA damage response [[78](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR78)]. Rap1 is a key telomere-capping protein that prevents non-homologous end joining and telomere fusion, and its overexpression causes histone loss and accelerates cellular senescence [[79](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR79), [80](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11118732/#CR80)]. Abstract
--------

Aging is a gradual and irreversible pathophysiological process. It presents with declines in tissue and cell functions and significant increases in the risks of various aging-related diseases, including neurodegenerative diseases, cardiovascular diseases, metabolic diseases, musculoskeletal diseases, and immune system diseases. Although the development of modern medicine has promoted human health and greatly extended life expectancy, with the aging of society, a variety of chronic diseases have gradually become the most important causes of disability and death in elderly individuals. Current research on aging focuses on elucidating how various endogenous and exogenous stresses (such as genomic instability, telomere dysfunction, epigenetic alterations, loss of proteostasis, compromise of autophagy, mitochondrial dysfunction, cellular senescence, stem cell exhaustion, altered intercellular communication, deregulated nutrient sensing) participate in the regulation of aging. Furthermore, thorough research on the pathogenesis of aging to identify interventions that promote health and longevity (such as caloric restriction, microbiota transplantation, and nutritional intervention) and clinical treatment methods for aging-related diseases (depletion of senescent cells, stem cell therapy, antioxidative and anti-inflammatory treatments, and hormone replacement therapy) could decrease the incidence and development of aging-related diseases and in turn promote healthy aging and longevity.

Introduction

Surprisingly, in 1925, research revealed that light intensity could impact the growth rate and lifespan of Drosophila.[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR1 "Northrop, J. H. The aging process is driven by a number of complex and important pathways, and many of those drivers are associated with chronic oxidative stress caused by elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS).[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR23 "Mossad, O. et al. Here, we will focus on recent findings on the biological processes of aging, highlight the important roles of aging in multiple aging-related diseases in humans, and discuss efficient interventions and treatments.

Molecular mechanisms of aging
-----------------------------

In 2013, López-Otín C et al. summarized aging in different organisms (especially mammals) as consisting of nine common features, including genomic instability, telomere attrition, epigenetic alteration, loss of proteostasis, deregulation of nutrient sensing, mitochondrial dysfunction, cellular senescence, stem cell exhaustion, and alteration of intercellular communication.[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR24 "Lopez-Otin, C., Blasco, M. A., Partridge, L., Serrano, M. & Kroemer, G. The hallmarks of aging. Cell 153, 1194–1217 (2013).") Over the years, the distinction between compromised autophagy and impaired proteostasis has enabled members of the aging field to propose a compromise of autophagy as the 10th hallmark of aging. This new concept was proposed by the main authors (Linda Partridge and Guido Kroemer) of a 2013 _Cell_ paper, in addition to other aging and autophagy researchers.[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR25 "Aman, Y. et al. Autophagy in healthy aging and disease. Nat. Aging 1, 634–650 (2021).") In recent years, scientists have made important achievements in research on signaling pathways and molecular mechanisms that affect the aging process. Here, we analyze and summarize these new advances at three different levels: the molecular level (genomic instability, telomere dysfunction, epigenetic alterations, loss of proteostasis, compromise of autophagy, mitochondrial dysfunction), the cellular level (cellular senescence, stem cell exhaustion, and intercellular communication), and systemic alterations (deregulated nutrient sensing). This information may broaden the understanding of multiple molecular signaling networks involved in the aging processes of organisms (Fig. [2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#Fig2)).

### Genomic instability

Genomic instability is an important cause of cellular senescence in many species.[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR26 "Aguilera, A. & Garcia-Muse, T. Causes of genome instability. Annu. Rev. Genet. 47, 1–32 (2013).") It leads to the deregulation of gene expression and consequently to impairment of cellular physiological aging, cessation of cell growth, and eventually cell death.[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR26 "Aguilera, A. & Garcia-Muse, T. Causes of genome instability. Annu. Rev. Genet. 47, 1–32 (2013).")

#### Nuclear DNA damage accumulation

Endogenous DNA damage is deemed a major marker of genomic instability, especially DNA double-strand breaks.[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR27 "Siametis, A., Niotis, G. & Garinis, G. A. DNA damage and the aging epigenome. J. Invest. Dermatol. 141, 961–967 (2021).") DNA damage activates the DNA damage response and cell cycle checkpoint pathways, such as the p53-p21 and p16INK4a-pRb pathways. Thus, the cell cycle is blocked to prevent the transmission of damaged genetic information to offspring cells.[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR28 "Williams, A. B. & Schumacher, B. p53 in the DNA-damage-repair process. Cold Spring Har. Perspect. Med. 6, a026070 (2016)."),[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR29 "Regulski, M. J. Cellular senescence: what, why, and how. Wounds 29, 168–174 (2017)."),[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR30 "Hou, J. et al. Aging with higher fractional exhaled nitric oxide levels are associated with increased urinary 8-oxo-7,8-dihydro-2’-deoxyguanosine concentrations in elder females. Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res. Int. 23, 23815–23824 (2016)."),[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR31 "Gorini, F. et al. The genomic landscape of 8-oxodG reveals enrichment at specific inherently fragile promoters. Nucleic Acids Res. 48, 4309–4324 (2020).") Tabula et al. performed single-cell RNA sequencing of more than 350,000 cells from male and female C57BL/6JN mice in six age groups ranging from 1 month (equivalent to human early childhood) to 30 months (equivalent to human centenarians).[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR32 "Tabula Muris, C. A single-cell transcriptomic atlas characterizes ageing tissues in the mouse. Nature 583, 590–595 (2020).") They analyzed changes in aging markers (including genomic instability) in 23 tissues and organs by Tabula Muris Senis, a tool capable of identifying aging-related changes in specific cell types.[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR32 "Tabula Muris, C. A single-cell transcriptomic atlas characterizes ageing tissues in the mouse. Nature 583, 590–595 (2020).") The researchers mapped the proportion of cells expressing each marker in all age groups and confirmed that the number of genomic mutations was positively associated with aging in all organs, with the tongue and bladder being the most affected.[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR32 "Tabula Muris, C. A single-cell transcriptomic atlas characterizes ageing tissues in the mouse. Nature 583, 590–595 (2020).") Analyses of centenarians have shown that long-lived individuals have fewer somatic and germ cell mutations than the general population, suggesting that they have more efficient DNA repair mechanisms to maintain genomic stability.[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR33 "Sanchez-Roman, I. et al. Molecular markers of DNA repair and brain metabolism correlate with cognition in centenarians. GeroScience 44, 103–125 (2022)."),[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR34 "Storci, G. et al. Persistent foci of nuclear DNA damage in senescent cells are also known as DNA segments with chromatin alterations reinforcing senescence (DNA-SCARSs).[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR35 "Gorgoulis, V. et al. Cellular senescence: defining a path forward. Cell 179, 813–827 (2019).") DNA-SCARSs are dynamic structures that may regulate multiple aspects of senescence in cells, including growth arrest and the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP).[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR35 "Gorgoulis, V. et al. Cellular senescence: defining a path forward. Cell 179, 813–827 (2019).") During cellular senescence, loss of the nuclear membrane protein Lamin B17,11,12 leads to impaired integrity of the nuclear envelope, causing chromatin fragment-containing nuclear membranes to enter the cytoplasm as cytoplasmic chromatin fragments (CCF).[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR36 "Dou, Z. et al. Cytoplasmic chromatin triggers inflammation in senescence and cancer. Nature 550, 402–406 (2017).") Cytoplasmic chromatin triggers inflammation in senescence and cancer. Nature 550, 402–406 (2017).")

Unlike nuclear DNA, mtDNA contains only exons and lacks histone protection and an effective gene repair system. In addition, considering the role of mitochondria as cellular powerhouses, some high-energy electron leakage may occur in the respiratory chain, which constitutes a major source of oxidative stress and subsequent elevated mutation rates in mtDNA.[38](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41392-022-01251-0#ref-CR38 "Son, J. M. & Lee, C. Aging: all roads lead to mitochondria. Semin. Cell Dev. Biol. 116, 160–168 (2021).") SUMMARY:
Physiological aging is observed in across many species, and is a degenerative process that occurs after maturation, including telomere attrition [10, 11], DNA damage [12, 13], mitochondrial dysfunction [14, 15], loss of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NAD+) levels [16, 17], impaired macro-autophagy [18, 19], stem cell exhaustion, inflammation [20, 21], loss of protein balance [22], deregulated nutrient-sensing [23], altered intercellular communication [24,25,26] and dysbiosis [27, 28], thereby leading to systemic functional decline. DNA damage and DNA damage response caused by the above factors can shock molecular processes and alter cell fate, such as cell death, senescence, and systemic breakdown of repair functions, eventually leading to the loss of cell and organ function and promoting the occurrence and development of age-related diseases

Abstract
--------

Aging is a complex and multifaceted process involving a variety of interrelated molecular mechanisms and cellular systems. Phenotypically, the biological aging process is accompanied by a gradual loss of cellular function and the systemic deterioration of multiple tissues, resulting in susceptibility to aging-related diseases. Emerging evidence suggests that aging is closely associated with telomere attrition, DNA damage, mitochondrial dysfunction, loss of nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide levels, impaired macro-autophagy, stem cell exhaustion, inflammation, loss of protein balance, deregulated nutrient sensing, altered intercellular communication, and dysbiosis. These age-related changes may be alleviated by intervention strategies, such as calorie restriction, improved sleep quality, enhanced physical activity, and targeted longevity genes. In this review, we summarise the key historical progress in the exploration of important causes of aging and anti-aging strategies in recent decades, which provides a basis for further understanding of the reversibility of aging phenotypes, the application prospect of synthetic biotechnology in anti-aging therapy is also prospected.

Aging drivers and age-related diseases. Major physiological features of aging include NAD+ loss, telomeres attrition, mitochondrial dysfunction, stem cell exhaustion, disabled macro-autophagy, DNA damage, protein balance loss, inflammation, dysbiosis, deregulated nutrient sensing, and altered cellular communication. These physiological characteristics of aging are primitive, antagonistic, and integrated, and their interaction promotes aging. When aging reaches a certain threshold, organ and tissue function continues to deteriorate, which increases the incidence and mortality of aging-related diseases, including cardiovascular, cerebrovascular, degenerative joint disease, diabetes, Parkinson’s disease, Alzheimer’s disease, and cancer

[Full size image](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1/figures/1)

In recent years, a large number of animal and clinical experiments have been conducted to study factors that induce aging, such as morphological and pathological changes and functional decline of the aging organism. Indeed, some differences between biological and chronological age reflect the validity of age-accelerated or deceleration procedures, which are well-known biomarkers of the aging process. Researchers have gradually expanded from traditional methods of measuring aging (including maximal energy expenditure at the respiratory, sensory, psychomotor, and cognitive levels) to modern biotechnological methods, such as genomics, epigenomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics. These techniques may have implications for assessing the spatiotemporal patterns of health degradation and effectiveness of anti-aging strategies.

Briefly, aging is a complex process, and its characteristics are interdependent. Each of these factors should be considered as an entry point for future exploration of the aging process and the development of novel life extensions. Here, we review the history and current state of aging research and summarise the characteristics of aging and the mechanisms promoting aging. In addition, we review different types of aging mechanisms and their corresponding anti-aging strategies. This knowledge can guide the design of preventive and therapeutic strategies to delay aging and age-related diseases and extend human health and longevity.

Potential triggers and molecular mechanisms of aging
----------------------------------------------------

Aging is a complex result of many biological processes, and many key factors trigger aging, such as DNA damage, telomere dysregulation, mitochondrial dysregulation, NAD+ loss, autophagy disorders, and stem cell exhaustion. Here, we summarise the main causes and underlying molecular mechanisms contributing to the aging process.

### Aging and DNA damage

DNA damage is a major internal factor that leads to genomic instability, epigenetic changes, protein stress, impaired mitochondrial function, and telomere dysfunction \[[12](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR12 "Zhao Y, et al. DNA damage and repair in age-related inflammation. Nat Rev Immunol. 2023;23(2):75–89.")\]. The continuous accumulation of DNA-damaged cells triggers cell death and senescence, ultimately leading to chronic inflammation, loss of function, atrophy, and disease in cells and tissues \[[40](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR40 "Gorbunova V, et al. The role of retrotransposable elements in ageing and age-associated diseases. Nature. 2021;596(7870):43–53.")\].

#### Molecular mechanisms of DNA damage

Genomic instability manifests as permanent and transmissible changes in DNA sequence \[[13](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR13 "Stead ER, Bjedov I. Balancing DNA repair to prevent ageing and cancer. Exp Cell Res. 2021;405(2):112679."), [41](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR41 "Peters A, Nawrot TS, Baccarelli AA. Hallm Environ Insults Cell. 2021;184(6):1455–68.")\]. DNA damage caused by an inherently unstable genome includes spontaneous deamination, hydrolysis, and many other chemical changes such as different types of breaks, changes in base positions, gaps, DNA-protein cross-links, and other subtle chemical modifications \[[12](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR12 "Zhao Y, et al. DNA damage and repair in age-related inflammation. Nat Rev Immunol. 2023;23(2):75–89."), [42](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR42 "Petr MA, et al. Protecting the aging genome. Trends Cell Biol. 2020;30(2):117–32.")\]. Abnormal DNA structures (e.g. G-quadruplexes, R-loops, and persistent single-stranded regions), as well as abnormal intermediates in DNA transactions (e.g. stalled transcription, replication, and recombination complexes), are considered phenotypes of DNA damage \[[13](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR13 "Stead ER, Bjedov I. Balancing DNA repair to prevent ageing and cancer. Exp Cell Res. 2021;405(2):112679.")\]. Genomic mutations caused by DNA instability adversely affect cellular functions and are major causes of cancer and genetic diseases. However, DNA instability is also the most important substrate in the evolution of species \[[43](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR43 "Carloni V, et al. The Adaptability of Chromosomal Instability in Cancer Therapy and Resistance. Int J Mol Sci. 2023;24(1):245."), [44](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR44 "Balzano E, Giunta S. Centromeres under Pressure: Evolutionary Innovation in Conflict with Conserved Function. Genes. 2020;11(8):912.")\]. DNA integrity is maintained by the continuous repair of highly complex DNA repair and DNA damage response (DDR) systems that counteract the time-dependent erosion and destruction of genetic DNA information. Progressive telomere shortening is another major contributor to DNA damage that accelerates the aging process \[[45](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR45 "Coluzzi E, Leone S, Sgura A. Oxidative Stress Induces Telomere Dysfunction and Senescence by Replication Fork Arrest. Cells. 2019;8(1):19.")\].

DNA damage is the major driver of age-related epigenetic changes. The epigenome, which includes DNA methylation and histone modifications, is unstable throughout the life cycle of somatic cells \[[46](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR46 "Tiwari M, Parvez S, Agrawala PK. Role of some epigenetic factors in DNA damage response pathway. AIMS Genet. 2017;4(1):69–83.")\]. DNA damage leads to persistent chromatin changes that enrich aging-enhancing DNA fragments (DNA-scars) in senescent cells \[[47](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR47 "Zheng GQ, Fu Y, He C. Nucleic acid oxidation in DNA damage repair and epigenetics. Chem Rev. 2014;114(8):4602–20.")\]. Persistent DNA damage and repair-related cellular physiological effects may leave epigenetic marks, resulting in epigenetic heterogeneity among cells. Transcription appears to change considerably more in senescent cells than in young cells. Thus, DDR may be a major cause of epigenetic changes that impair gene expression control, leading to somatic heterogeneity and a time-dependent decline in overall function.

#### Relationship between DNA damage and aging

During aging, numerous exogenous and endogenous genotoxins, photoaging, and mechanical stress in tissues continuously induce DNA damage (Fig. [2](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#Fig2)). Approximately 105 DNA damage events occur in mammalian cells every day, although most of the DNA damage is effectively excised or repaired. Notably, a small portion escapes the DNA damage detection and repair system, subsequently resulting in failure to repair or repair errors \[[48](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR48 "Ping Y, Zhifang LI, Wen C. Epigenetic mechanisms of DNA damage and repair. J Med Mol Biology. 2009;6(5):459–62.")\]. Many studies using mammalian models have confirmed an inextricable link between DNA damage and aging \[[49](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR49 "Fischer KE, Riddle NC. Sex differences in aging: genomic instability. Journals Gerontol Ser a-Biological Sci Med Sci. 2018;73(2):166–74."),[50](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR50 "Li WT, et al. Epigenetic Regulation of Nucleotide Excision Repair. Front Cell Dev Biology. 2022;10:847051."),[51](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR51 "Zhang JW. Brothers in arms: emerging roles of RNA epigenetics in DNA damage repair. Cell Bioscience. 2017;7(1):24."),[52](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR52 "Kaufmann W. Epigenetics and the DNA damage response. Environ Mol Mutagen. 2012;53:S27-27.")\]. As aging progresses, the DNA repair capacity gradually declines, and the increased molecular phenotype of genomic instability becomes the main marker of aging. Markers of DNA damage are found in patients with age-related diseases such as cardiovascular disease \[[53](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR53 "Shukla PC, et al. DNA damage repair and cardiovascular diseases. Can J Cardiol. 2010;26:A13-6.")\], Alzheimer’s disease \[[54](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR54 "Lin XZ, et al. Contributions of DNA Damage to Alzheimer’s Disease. Int J Mol Sci. 2020;21(5):1666.")\], and cancer \[[55](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR55 "Alhmoud JF, et al. DNA Damage/Repair Manage Cancers. Advances in Medical Biochemistry, Genomics, Physiology, and Pathology. 2021:309–39.")\], suggesting that DNA damage is directly related to the incidence of these diseases. Patients with genetic or acquired defects in DNA repair proteins also exhibit features of premature aging and that differences in the location of the defect in the DNA repair system can lead to premature aging in different organs \[[56](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR56 "Lu T, Xu K. The multifunction of mismatch repair protein. Chem Life. 2008;28(6):696–700.")\]. Specifically, RecQ helicase plays an important role in DNA recombination, replication, repair, and telomere maintenance, and its mutation may increase the incidence of Werner, Bloom, and Rothmund-Thomson syndromes \[[57](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR57 "Monnat RJ. Human RECQ helicases: roles in DNA metabolism, mutagenesis and cancer biology. Sem Cancer Biol. 2010;20(5):329–39.")\]. Global genome nucleotide excision repair deficiency leads to a thousand-fold increase in skin cancer susceptibility and may accelerate neurodegeneration \[[58](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR58 "Diderich K, Alanazi M, Hoeijmakers JHJ. Premature aging and cancer in nucleotide excision repair-disorders. DNA Repair. 2011;10(7):772–80.")\]. Impaired transcription-coupled repair mechanisms can cause typical age-related pathologies, such as neurodegeneration, osteoporosis, and atherosclerosis \[[59](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR59 "Sepe S, et al. Nucleotide excision repair in chronic neurodegenerative diseases. DNA Repair. 2013;12(8):568–77.")\]. Hutchinson-Gilford progeria is associated with nuclear genome instability, defects in DNA double-strand break repair leading to telangiectasia and Nijmegen break syndrome, and defects in DNA cross-linking repair leading to anaemia \[[60](https://biosignaling.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12964-024-01663-1#ref-CR60 "Xu SW, Jin ZG. Hutchinson-Gilford Progeria Syndrome: Cardiovascular pathologies and potential therapies. Trends Biochem Sci. 2019;44(7):561–4.")\]. In addition, DNA damage caused by mitochondrial defects is another underlying factor in a class of progressive diseases that affect multiple organs.

SUMMARY:
We propose the following twelve hallmarks of aging: genomic instability, telomere attrition, epigenetic alterations, loss of proteostasis, disabled macroautophagy, deregulated nutrient-sensing, mitochondrial dysfunction, cellular senescence, stem cell exhaustion, altered intercellular communication, chronic inflammation, and dysbiosis. The scheme compiles the 12 hallmarks of aging proposed in this work: genomic instability, telomere attrition, epigenetic alterations, loss of proteostasis, disabled macroautophagy, deregulated nutrient-sensing, mitochondrial dysfunction, cellular senescence, stem cell exhaustion, altered intercellular communication, chronic inflammation, and dysbiosis. Several hallmarks of aging (i.e., genomic instability, epigenetic alterations, chronic inflammation, and dysbiosis) are very similar to specific cancer hallmarks and hence constitute common “meta-hallmarks,” while other features of aging (i.e., telomere attrition and stem cell exhaustion) act likely to suppress oncogenesis and hence can be viewed as preponderantly “antagonistic hallmarks.” Disabled macroautophagy and cellular senescence are two hallmarks of aging that exert context-dependent oncosuppressive and pro-tumorigenic effects.

Summary
-------

Aging is driven by hallmarks fulfilling the following three premises: (1) their age-associated manifestation, (2) the acceleration of aging by experimentally accentuating them, and (3) the opportunity to decelerate, stop, or reverse aging by therapeutic interventions on them. We propose the following twelve hallmarks of aging: [genomic instability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genome-instability "Learn more about genomic instability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [telomere](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/telomere "Learn more about telomere from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") attrition, epigenetic alterations, loss of [proteostasis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-homeostasis "Learn more about proteostasis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), disabled [macroautophagy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/macroautophagy "Learn more about macroautophagy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), deregulated nutrient-sensing, mitochondrial dysfunction, cellular senescence, stem cell exhaustion, altered intercellular communication, chronic inflammation, and dysbiosis. These hallmarks are interconnected among each other, as well as to the recently proposed hallmarks of health, which include organizational features of spatial compartmentalization, maintenance of [homeostasis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/homeostasis "Learn more about homeostasis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and adequate responses to stress.

Introduction
------------

Aging research explores the decline in function of organisms during adulthood. Since 2013, when the first edition of the hallmarks of aging was published in _Cell_,[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib1) close to 300,000 articles dealing with this subject have been published, which is as many as during the preceding century. Hence, time has become ripe for a new edition of the hallmarks of aging incorporating the main knowledge obtained a decade on.

The distinction among “hallmarks” is intrinsically diffuse, since they interact and are not independent of each other. Therefore, their classification is inevitably arbitrary, but we proposed three criteria that must apply for each hallmark of aging: (1) the time-dependent manifestation of alterations accompanying the aging process, (2) the possibility to accelerate aging by experimentally accentuating the hallmark, and—most decisively—(3) the opportunity to decelerate, halt, or reverse aging by therapeutic interventions on the hallmark. Rather than elaborating a compendium of age-associated alterations, we shall focus on the molecular, cellular, and systemic processes mechanistically accounting for their manifestation. That said, both in laboratory animals and in human medicine, objective quantification of morphological and functional decline affecting the aging organism is essential to measure biological aging. Indeed, disparity between biological and [chronological age](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/age "Learn more about chronological age from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can reflect the efficacy of age-accelerating or -decelerating manipulations that evaluate the contribution of a given hallmark to the aging process. For this reason, standardized physiological measurements (e.g., respirometry to measure basal and maximal energy expenditure), functional tests (e.g., at the sensory, psychomotor, and cognitive levels), and ever more sophisticated “omics” technologies (e.g., genomics, [epigenomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epigenomics "Learn more about epigenomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [transcriptomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcriptomics "Learn more about transcriptomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [proteomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/proteomics "Learn more about proteomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and metabolomics), often applied at the single-cell level, are instrumental for evaluating the spatiotemporal patterns of health degradation and the (in)efficacy of anti-aging strategies.

In 2013, we suggested nine molecular, cellular, and systemic hallmarks of aging: DNA instability, [telomere](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/telomere "Learn more about telomere from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") attrition, epigenetic alterations, loss of [proteostasis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/protein-homeostasis "Learn more about proteostasis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), deregulated nutrient-sensing, mitochondrial dysfunction, cellular senescence, stem cell exhaustion, and altered intercellular communication.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib1) Recent research has confirmed and extended the importance of all these hallmarks. They have withstood scrutiny by tens of thousands of aging researchers, but they require an update to deal with the discoveries of the last decade. For example, in 2013, much of the evidence on anti-aging interventions was limited to non-mammalian model organisms including yeast, nematodes, and fruit flies. Fortunately, experiments involving mice (and in some cases, non-human primates) have now corroborated the validity of most of these hallmarks in mammals. Of note, human age-related diseases have statistically higher chances to co-occur and to share genomic characteristics when they are causally linked to the same hallmark rather than to different hallmarks,[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib2) clinically validating the approach that we have chosen.

Besides the necessary update of the previous hallmarks, we have also introduced some reorganizations and included the following three additional hallmarks of aging: disabled [macroautophagy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/macroautophagy "Learn more about macroautophagy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), chronic inflammation, and dysbiosis. Disabled macroautophagy was initially treated as a special case of loss of proteostasis. However, macroautophagy does not only affect proteins but can target entire organelles and non-proteinaceous [macromolecules](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/macromolecule "Learn more about macromolecules from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), justifying its discussion as a separate entity. Moreover, we considered that the final hallmark that we listed in 2013, altered intercellular communication, was too vast, requiring a separate discussion of chronic inflammation and age-associated dysbiosis ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#fig1)).

Figure 1. The hallmarks of aging

The scheme compiles the 12 hallmarks of aging proposed in this work: genomic instability, telomere attrition, epigenetic alterations, loss of proteostasis, disabled macroautophagy, deregulated nutrient-sensing, mitochondrial dysfunction, cellular senescence, stem cell exhaustion, altered intercellular communication, chronic inflammation, and dysbiosis. These hallmarks are grouped into three categories: primary, antagonistic, and integrative.

The interdependence of aging hallmarks means that the experimental accentuation or attenuation of one specific hallmark usually affects other hallmarks as well. This underscores the fact that aging is a complex process that has to be conceived as a whole. Accordingly, each of the hallmarks should be considered as a point-of-entry for future exploration of the aging process, as well as for the development of new anti-aging medicines.

Genomic instability
-------------------

Genome integrity and stability are pervasively threatened by exogenous chemical, physical, and biological agents, as well as by endogenous challenges such as [DNA replication](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-replication "Learn more about DNA replication from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") errors, [chromosome segregation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/chromosome-segregation "Learn more about chromosome segregation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") defects, oxidative processes, and spontaneous hydrolytic reactions. The wide range of [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") lesions caused by these extrinsic or intrinsic sources of damage include [point mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/point-mutation "Learn more about point mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), deletions, translocations, [telomere shortening](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/telomere-shortening "Learn more about telomere shortening from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), single- and double-strand breaks, [chromosomal rearrangements](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/chromosomal-rearrangement "Learn more about chromosomal rearrangements from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), defects in nuclear architecture, and [gene disruption](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/gene-disruption "Learn more about gene disruption from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") caused by the integration of viruses or [transposons](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transposable-element "Learn more about transposons from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). All these molecular alterations and the resulting genomic mosaicism may contribute to both normal and pathological aging.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib3) Accordingly, organisms have evolved a complex array of [DNA repair](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-repair "Learn more about DNA repair from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and maintenance mechanisms to deal with the damage inflicted to nuclear and mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) and to ensure the appropriate chromosomal architecture and stability. These DNA repair networks lose efficiency with age, which accentuates the accumulation of genomic damage and the ectopic accumulation of DNA in the cytosol[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib4) ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#fig2)A).

Figure 2. Loss of cellular integrity caused by genomic instability, telomere attrition, and epigenetic alterations

(A) Endogenous or exogenous agents cause a variety of DNA lesions that contribute to both normal and pathological aging. Such lesions can by repaired by a variety of mechanisms that lose efficiency with age. Excessive DNA damage, insufficient DNA repair, alterations in nuclear architecture, and telomere attrition favor the aging process. BER, [base excision repair](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/base-excision-repair "Learn more about base excision repair from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); HR, [homologous recombination](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/homologous-recombination "Learn more about homologous recombination from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); NER, [nucleotide excision repair](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nucleotide-excision-repair "Learn more about nucleotide excision repair from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); [NHEJ](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/non-homologous-end-joining "Learn more about NHEJ from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), non-homologous end joining; MMR, [mismatch repair](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-mismatch-repair "Learn more about mismatch repair from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); SAC, [spindle assembly checkpoint](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/spindle-checkpoint "Learn more about spindle assembly checkpoint from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); TERT, [telomerase reverse transcriptase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/telomerase-reverse-transcriptase "Learn more about telomerase reverse transcriptase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); TLS, _trans_-lesion synthesis.

(B) Changes in the acetylation and [methylation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/methylation "Learn more about methylation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of DNA or histones, as well as in levels or activity of chromatin-associated proteins or of non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs) induce epigenetic changes that contribute to the aging process. The red portions of the hourglasses indicate age-associated alterations and the blue portions strategies for avoiding them.

### Nuclear DNA

Cells from aged humans and model organisms accumulate [somatic mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/somatic-mutation "Learn more about somatic mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") at nuclear DNA.[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib5) Other forms of damage, such as chromosomal [aneuploidy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/aneuploidy "Learn more about aneuploidy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and copy-number variations, are also associated with aging. All these DNA alterations may affect essential genes and transcriptional pathways, resulting in dysfunctional cells that may finally compromise tissue and organismal [homeostasis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/homeostasis "Learn more about homeostasis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This is especially relevant when DNA damage impacts on stem cells, hampering their role in tissue renewal or leading to their exhaustion, which in turn promotes aging and increases susceptibility to age-related pathologies.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib6),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib7) The mutational burden in histologically [normal human](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/normal-human "Learn more about normal human from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") tissues is remarkable. For example, normal esophageal epithelium cells from young individuals already display hundreds of mutations and may carry more than 2,000 mutations per cell by middle age.[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib8) The accumulation of [DNA mutations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-mutation "Learn more about DNA mutations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") throughout life is likely tolerated because of the excessive energetic cost of the complete repair of all genomic damages caused by exogenous and endogenous challenges. Consequently, cells favor survival over genomic integrity.[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib9) These data also suggest that similar to carcinogenesis, driver mutations alone may not be sufficient to accelerate aging because they require a permissive microenvironment created by non-mutagenic promoting factors to become penetrant.[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib10)

Comparative analysis of the mutational landscape across mammalian species has shown that species-specific somatic [mutation rate](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mutation-rate "Learn more about mutation rate from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is inversely correlated with lifespan.[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib11) To date, there is no clear evidence that the normal rate of mutation fixation is responsible for aging, but numerous studies have shown that DNA repair deficiencies have the potential to cause aging. Thus, alterations in DNA repair mechanisms accelerate aging in mice and underlie several human progeroid syndromes.[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib12) Conversely, [transgenic mice](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transgenic-mouse "Learn more about transgenic mice from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") overexpressing the [mitotic checkpoint](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/spindle-checkpoint "Learn more about mitotic checkpoint from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") kinase BubR1 exhibit an extended healthy lifespan[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib13) ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#tbl1)). Moreover, studies in humans and other long-lived species have shown that enhanced DNA repair mechanisms coevolve with increased longevity.[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib14) Sirtuin-6 (SIRT6) may play a major role in this differential reparative efficiency across species. Overexpression of SIRT6 in mice reduces genomic instability, improves double-strand break repair, and extends lifespan[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib15) ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#tbl1)), although other explanations, such as improved [glucose metabolism](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/glucose-metabolism "Learn more about glucose metabolism from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and restoration of energy homeostasis, have been proposed to explain the prolongevity effects of SIRT6.[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib16) Notably, recent work has shown that small-molecule activation of 8-oxoguanine [DNA glycosylase](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/dna-glycosylase "Learn more about DNA glycosylase from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") 1 increases oxidative DNA damage repair and may have therapeutic applications in the context of aging and other processes linked to excessive oxidative damage.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867422013770#bib17) These findings suggest that interventions aimed at reducing the [mutational load](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mutational-load "Learn more about mutational load from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of nuclear DNA or at enhancing or rerouting its repair mechanisms may delay aging and the onset of age-related diseases, but further causal evidence in this regard is still missing.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Aging involves complex interactions among multiple biological processes, including genomic instability, mitochondrial dysfunction, and cellular senescence, leading to systemic functional decline.

enriched_points.point_title: Role of DNA damage and telomere attrition
enriched_points.expanded_content: Accumulation of DNA damage and progressive telomere shortening contribute significantly to cellular senescence and genomic instability, driving the aging process and related diseases.
Mitochondrial dysfunction leads to reduced energy production and increased oxidative stress, disrupting cellular homeostasis and accelerating aging and age-associated pathologies.
Maintaining protein homeostasis is crucial for cellular function; loss of proteostasis results in protein aggregation, contributing to cellular dysfunction and aging.
Cellular senescence, a stable cell cycle arrest, plays a dual role in aging—protecting against cancer but promoting tissue dysfunction and inflammation when accumulated.
